content.text: React imposes no folder structure. The scaffolding tools (Vite, CRA, Next.js) provide a minimal starting point, but how you organise everything beyond

is entirely up to you. The right structure depends on project size: a small project benefits from simplicity; a large one benefits from locality — keeping related files together so you can delete a feature by deleting a folder.

attrs.alt: Three React project structure patterns: flat by type (components/, hooks/, pages/ at top level), feature/domain (features/auth/, features/dashboard/ with shared/), and collocated (each component in its own folder with test and CSS alongside it)
attrs.title: React project structure patterns

content.text: A fresh Vite React-TS project looks like this. Every file has a specific role:

my-app/
├── public/
│   └── vite.svg                 ← static files served as-is at /
├── src/
│   ├── assets/
│   │   └── react.svg            ← imported assets, hashed in build
│   ├── App.css                  ← styles scoped to App
│   ├── App.tsx                  ← root component
│   ├── index.css                ← global styles
│   ├── main.tsx                 ← entry point: createRoot().render()
│   └── vite-env.d.ts            ← Vite's environment type declarations
├── index.html                   ← HTML entry (unique to Vite)
├── vite.config.ts
├── tsconfig.json
├── tsconfig.node.json
└── package.json
are copied to the output directory unchanged and referenced by absolute URL (
are processed by the bundler — they get content-hashed filenames and can be imported in TypeScript.
, and so on at the top level. This is the default that most tutorials and many teams start with.

src/
├── components/     ← reusable UI pieces
│   ├── Button.tsx
│   ├── Input.tsx
│   └── Modal.tsx
├── hooks/          ← custom React hooks
│   ├── useAuth.ts
│   └── useFetch.ts
├── pages/          ← route-level components (Vite + React Router)
│   ├── HomePage.tsx
│   └── AboutPage.tsx
├── services/       ← API calls, external integrations
│   └── api.ts
├── store/          ← global state (Redux / Zustand)
│   └── index.ts
├── types/          ← shared TypeScript interfaces
│   └── models.ts
├── utils/          ← pure functions with no React dependency
│   └── formatDate.ts
├── App.tsx
└── main.tsx
immediately obvious where a file type goes; minimal nesting; works well up to ~30 components.
each folder grows huge as the project scales; deleting a feature requires hunting across multiple folders.
Group files by the feature they belong to, not by their technical type. A feature folder contains its components, hooks, types, and state — everything you need to reason about or delete that feature.

src/
├── features/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── components/
│   │   │   ├── LoginForm.tsx
│   │   │   └── SignupForm.tsx
│   │   ├── hooks/
│   │   │   └── useAuth.ts
│   │   ├── store/
│   │   │   └── authSlice.ts
│   │   ├── types.ts
│   │   └── index.ts         ← public API of this feature
│   ├── cart/
│   │   ├── CartDrawer.tsx
│   │   ├── useCart.ts
│   │   └── index.ts
│   └── products/
│       ├── ProductList.tsx
│       ├── ProductCard.tsx
│       └── index.ts
├── shared/
│   ├── components/          ← used by multiple features
│   │   ├── Button.tsx
│   │   └── Modal.tsx
│   └── utils/
│       └── formatDate.ts
├── App.tsx
└── main.tsx
; teams can own features independently; boundaries are explicit.
requires deciding what counts as a "feature" upfront; can be overkill for small apps.
Give each component its own folder containing the implementation, styles, test, and a barrel index. This keeps related files together while maintaining the flat

src/components/
├── Button/
│   ├── Button.tsx
│   ├── Button.module.css
│   ├── Button.test.tsx
│   └── index.ts             ← re-exports Button as default
├── Modal/
│   ├── Modal.tsx
│   ├── Modal.module.css
│   ├── Modal.test.tsx
│   └── index.ts
└── DataTable/
    ├── DataTable.tsx
    ├── DataTableRow.tsx     ← sub-component private to DataTable
    ├── DataTable.test.tsx
    └── index.ts
barrel re-exports the public API so consumers import from
Always index.ts (not index.tsx unless JSX is exported)
Deeply nested structures produce brittle relative imports like
. Configure an alias in both Vite and TypeScript so you can write
After this configuration every file in the project can import shared utilities as
without caring how many levels deep the consumer lives.
A consistent internal structure within each component file makes reading faster. A common ordering:
What to Avoid: Barrel File Pitfalls
that re-export everything) can make imports cleaner, but large barrel files slow down build tools because the bundler must process the entire barrel to determine which exports are used:
Keep barrel files scoped to what they describe (one per feature, one per component folder) rather than one mega-barrel for all components. Vite's module graph handles individual imports efficiently.
Easy to find; shipped to users unless excluded in tsconfig
Easy to exclude from production build; harder to co-locate with source
Most modern React projects use collocated tests. The bundler excludes
files from production bundles automatically because they are never imported by the application code.
A useful pattern for testability is separating
(no side effects, pure render from props) from
(fetch data, manage state, connect to stores). This is not a strict rule but helps when components become hard to test:
Both Vite and CRA support layered environment files. Files are merged in priority order (higher overrides lower):
file listing all required variables with placeholder values. Developers clone the repo, copy it to
Feature folders for a five-component app add friction with no benefit. Start flat, refactor to features when a folder exceeds ~15 files.
in the same project causes confusion and breaks imports on case-sensitive file systems (Linux vs macOS difference). Pick PascalCase for components and stick to it.
Feature A importing from Feature B which imports from Feature A causes bundler warnings and subtle runtime bugs. The rule: features may import from
but not from other features. Cross-feature communication goes through shared state or events.
Putting all types in a single types.ts.
grows without bound and creates hidden coupling. Co-locate types with the feature that owns them; export them from that feature's
already includes these — never override it.
With React Router (client-side routing in a Vite/CRA project) there are two main patterns for where to put route-level components:

# Option A — dedicated pages/ folder
src/
├── pages/
│   ├── HomePage.tsx          ← maps to /
│   ├── ProductListPage.tsx   ← maps to /products
│   └── ProductDetailPage.tsx ← maps to /products/:id
└── components/               ← reusable across pages
    ├── ProductCard.tsx
    └── SearchBar.tsx

# Option B — feature-collocated routing (React Router data router style)
src/
└── features/
    └── products/
        ├── ProductListPage.tsx
        ├── ProductDetailPage.tsx
        ├── loader.ts          ← React Router loader function
        └── action.ts          ← React Router action function

Option A is simpler to understand at first glance. Option B becomes attractive with the React Router 6.4+ data router since each feature folder owns its loader and action alongside its page component.
Where global state lives depends on the library:
Feature slices import from their own folder; combined in store/index.ts
One file per store; can live inside the feature that owns it
Collocate with the feature when only one feature uses it
Query hooks live inside the feature that fetches the data

src/
├── assets/
│   ├── images/           ← imported in components (bundler adds content hash)
│   │   └── logo.svg
│   └── fonts/            ← @font-face sources
│       └── Inter.woff2
├── constants/
│   ├── routes.ts         ← route path strings ('/products/:id')
│   └── config.ts         ← app-wide settings (pagination size, etc.)
└── lib/
    ├── axios.ts          ← pre-configured Axios instance
    └── dayjs.ts          ← dayjs setup with locale and plugins
prevents typos and makes renaming a route a single-file change:
A folder has more than ~15 files.
Time to split into sub-folders or features.
You frequently edit multiple files to change one behaviour.
A new team member cannot find where something is without asking.
Multiple teams are touching the same files.
Relative imports are unambiguous but fragile when files move. Beyond the
alias you can define multiple named aliases for common sub-directories:
Now an import that would be

# Dependencies — never commit
node_modules/

# Build output — generated; reproducible from source
dist/
.next/          # Next.js
build/          # CRA

# Vite cache — fast restore on next start
.vite/

# Local environment variables — secrets, per-developer
.env.local
.env*.local

# Editor and OS artifacts
.DS_Store
*.local
.idea/
.vscode/        # usually committed if it contains shared launch configs

# TypeScript build info — regenerated by tsc
tsconfig.tsbuildinfo
If you are using VS Code, you may want to commit a
with team-wide settings (formatter, lint-on-save) while keeping
to recommend extensions. In that case remove
from the gitignore and add only the machine-specific files like

src/
├── features/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── LoginPage.tsx
│   │   ├── useAuth.ts
│   │   └── index.ts
│   ├── products/
│   │   ├── ProductListPage.tsx
│   │   ├── ProductDetailPage.tsx
│   │   ├── ProductCard.tsx          ← used only within products feature
│   │   ├── useProducts.ts
│   │   └── index.ts
│   └── cart/
│       ├── CartDrawer.tsx
│       ├── CartItem.tsx
│       ├── useCart.ts
│       └── index.ts
├── shared/
│   ├── components/
│   │   ├── Button/
│   │   │   ├── Button.tsx
│   │   │   ├── Button.module.css
│   │   │   ├── Button.test.tsx
│   │   │   └── index.ts
│   │   └── Layout/
│   │       ├── Layout.tsx
│   │       └── index.ts
│   ├── hooks/
│   │   └── usePagination.ts
│   └── utils/
│       ├── formatCurrency.ts
│       └── formatDate.ts
├── constants/
│   └── routes.ts
├── store/
│   └── index.ts                     ← Zustand / Redux root
├── App.tsx
└── main.tsx
Start flat (by type) for small projects; migrate to feature grouping when folders exceed ~15 files.
for hooks, camelCase for everything else.
alias in Vite and tsconfig to eliminate relative imports across folder boundaries.
Collocate tests and styles with the component they test —
Barrel files (index.ts re-exports) simplify imports but should be scoped to one folder — avoid a single barrel for the entire project.
